title: Quiz: Quantum Simulation Applications
description: Test your understanding of quantum simulation techniques and applications

questions.question: Why is simulating quantum systems on classical computers exponentially hard?

options.text: The Hilbert space dimension grows as 2^n for n qubits

questions.explanation: The Hilbert space dimension grows exponentially (2^n) with the number of qubits, requiring exponentially more memory and computation classically.

questions.question: What is Trotter decomposition used for?

options.text: Approximating exp(-iHt) when H is a sum of non-commuting terms

questions.explanation: Trotter decomposition breaks exp(-i(A+B)t) into products of exp(-iAt) and exp(-iBt), enabling simulation of Hamiltonians that are sums of local terms.

questions.question: What is the error scaling for first-order Trotter decomposition?
questions.explanation: First-order Trotter has error O(t²/n) where t is total time and n is the number of steps. This comes from the commutator of the Hamiltonian terms.

questions.question: Second-order Trotter (Strang splitting) requires fewer steps than first-order for the same accuracy.
questions.explanation: True. Second-order Trotter has O(t³/n²) error, so for fixed error it needs O(√n) fewer steps than first-order's O(t²/n).

questions.question: Which spin model has the Hamiltonian H = -J Σ Z_i Z_{i+1} - h Σ X_i?
questions.explanation: The transverse-field Ising model has ZZ interactions along one axis and a transverse field in X, making it exactly solvable in 1D but showing quantum phase transitions.

questions.question: What limits quantum simulation depth on NISQ devices?

options.text: Gate errors accumulating with circuit depth

questions.explanation: Gate errors accumulate with depth. A circuit with depth D and error rate ε per gate has fidelity approximately (1-ε)^D, which decreases exponentially.

questions.question: What observable indicates entanglement spreading in a quantum quench?
questions.explanation: Entanglement entropy across a bipartition measures quantum correlations. After a quench, it typically grows (often linearly in time for chaotic systems) until saturating.

questions.question: Which are error mitigation strategies for quantum simulation? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Zero-noise extrapolation, symmetry verification, and randomized compiling are error mitigation techniques. Simply using more qubits doesn't reduce errors.
